network.overall_strategy: Two experimentally demonstrated prebiotic routes converge on L-threonine: (1) a cyanosulfidic surface pathway starting from lactaldehyde and HCN via cyanohydrin and aminonitrile intermediates (Patel et al., 2015), and (2) a hydrothermal aldol condensation of L-alanine with formaldehyde (Aubrey et al., 2008). The network traces these pathways back to simple C1 starting materials (CO2, H2, HCN, NH3) through hydrothermal carbon fixation, surface aldol and formose chemistry, and cyanosulfidic nitrile transformations. Key hub molecules—formaldehyde, pyruvate, L-alanine, and ammonia—interconnect the hydrothermal, surface, and biochemical environments, creating a robust, redundant network with multiple independent routes to threonine.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich fluids from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is reduced to formate (HCOO⁻) via hydride transfer on the greigite surface, which acts as a structural analog of biological [Fe4S4] clusters. The reaction is thermodynamically favorable under the strongly reducing conditions of alkaline hydrothermal vents.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate is well established in hydrothermal prebiotic chemistry. Greigite's structural similarity to biological iron-sulfur clusters supports its role as an abiotic CO2 fixation catalyst (Roldan et al., 2015; Herschy et al., 2014). Formate is the first stable C1 intermediate in hydrothermal carbon fixation.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of formaldehyde from CO2

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type conditions in submarine hydrothermal systems

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is first reduced to CO on magnetite/native iron surfaces, followed by further hydrogenation to formaldehyde via Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) surface chemistry. The reaction proceeds through adsorbed CO intermediates on the mineral surface that are progressively hydrogenated.
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde production from CO2 and H2 via Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite and native iron surfaces has been documented in hydrothermal systems. Formaldehyde is identified as a key C1 electrophile abundantly available in hydrothermal vent fluids (Aubrey et al., 2008; McCollom & Seewald, 2007). This supplies the C1 building block required for both the formose pathway (Pathway 1) and the aldol condensation with alanine (Pathway 2).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions; multi-step reductive carbon fixation analogous to rTCA cycle

reactions.mechanism: Sequential CO2 reduction and C-C bond formation on iron-sulfur mineral surfaces produces pyruvate through formate and acetate intermediates, analogous to the reductive tricarboxylic acid (rTCA) cycle. This involves stepwise reduction of CO2 to C1, C2, and C3 units on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal synthesis of pyruvate from CO2 and H2 on iron-sulfur and iron-oxide mineral surfaces has been demonstrated as part of a prebiotic analog of the rTCA cycle (Muchowska et al., 2017; Varma et al., 2018). Pyruvate is a central metabolic hub molecule that feeds into amino acid synthesis. Its formation under hydrothermal conditions provides the C3 backbone for alanine and, ultimately, threonine.

reactions.name: Formose reaction: formaldehyde self-condensation to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: Alkaline evaporitic pool conditions; borate stabilizes sugar intermediates

reactions.mechanism: Aldol self-condensation of two formaldehyde molecules to produce glycolaldehyde (the simplest sugar). The reaction is base-catalyzed: OH⁻ deprotonates formaldehyde hydrate to form a nucleophilic enolate that attacks a second formaldehyde molecule. Borate minerals stabilize the glycolaldehyde product against further uncontrolled condensation.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction producing glycolaldehyde from formaldehyde is well-established prebiotic chemistry (Breslow, 1959; Ricardo et al., 2004). Borate minerals stabilize sugars and prevent the runaway polymerization that plagues the classical formose reaction (Ricardo et al., 2004). Glycolaldehyde is a key C2 building block for sugar and aldehyde chain extension, and serves as the precursor to lactaldehyde needed for Pathway 1 threonine synthesis.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glycolaldehyde with formaldehyde to lactaldehyde

conditions.other: Alkaline evaporitic pool conditions; clay surfaces concentrate and orient reactants

reactions.mechanism: Base-catalyzed aldol condensation: the alpha-carbon of glycolaldehyde is deprotonated to form an enolate nucleophile, which attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of formaldehyde. The product is lactaldehyde (2-hydroxypropionaldehyde), a C3 hydroxy-aldehyde. Montmorillonite clay surfaces adsorb and concentrate the reactants.
reactions.reasoning: Lactaldehyde is a prebiotically plausible C3 aldol product available from aldol chemistry of simpler aldehydes (glycolaldehyde + formaldehyde) under cyanosulfidic conditions (Patel et al., 2015). This reaction connects the formose chemistry to the cyanosulfidic threonine pathway. Lactaldehyde serves as the critical aldehyde that introduces the methyl branch distinguishing threonine from serine.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation from lactaldehyde and HCN

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic conditions in surface evaporitic pools; mildly basic pH buffered by phosphate

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide ion (CN⁻) to the electrophilic aldehyde carbonyl carbon of lactaldehyde. The cyanide ion attacks the carbonyl carbon, and subsequent protonation of the resulting alkoxide yields the cyanohydrin. This is a classic Kiliani-type cyanohydrin reaction that extends the carbon chain by one unit while introducing a nitrile functional group.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is the first step of the cyanosulfidic threonine synthesis pathway demonstrated by Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015, Nature Chemistry). HCN-derived cyanohydrin formation is a central transformation in the broader Powell-Sutherland cyanosulfidic chemistry framework. Phosphate minerals buffer pH and may facilitate the reaction.

reactions.name: Strecker-type amination of cyanohydrin to aminonitrile

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic conditions; UV irradiation may assist the amination; wet-dry cycling concentrates reactants

reactions.mechanism: Strecker-type amination: ammonia displaces the hydroxyl group at the alpha-carbon adjacent to the nitrile, converting the cyanohydrin to an aminonitrile with release of water. The reaction involves nucleophilic attack of NH3 on the carbon bearing the hydroxyl group, with rearrangement to yield the C4 aminonitrile with the beta-hydroxy group characteristic of threonine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the second step of the cyanosulfidic threonine synthesis demonstrated by Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry). The Strecker-type amination is a well-established transformation in prebiotic aminonitrile chemistry. The reaction parallels the broader cyanosulfidic strategy where ammonia amination of cyanohydrins is a general route to aminonitrile amino acid precursors.

reactions.name: Nitrile hydrolysis of 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile to L-threonine

conditions.other: Wet-dry cycling in evaporitic pools; clay surfaces may catalyze hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydrolysis: water first attacks the nitrile carbon of 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile, forming an amide intermediate (2-amino-3-hydroxybutanamide). Further hydrolysis of the amide yields the carboxylic acid (threonine) with release of ammonia. This is the terminal step of the cyanosulfidic aminonitrile-to-amino acid conversion.
reactions.reasoning: This is the final step of the cyanosulfidic threonine pathway demonstrated by Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry). Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acid via amide intermediate is well-established chemistry. The released ammonia recycles into the network as a reactant for further amination reactions, creating a catalytic loop. Note that this step produces racemic threonine; the origin of homochirality remains an open question.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate to L-alanine

conditions.other: 70°C, iron-catalyzed reductive amination with hydroxylamine/ammonia as nitrogen source

reactions.mechanism: Reductive amination: ammonia attacks the ketone carbonyl of pyruvate to form an imine (Schiff base) intermediate. The imine is then reduced by Fe⁰ (or Fe²⁺/H₂) to yield L-alanine. Iron serves as both the reducing agent and Lewis acid catalyst. The reaction converts the alpha-keto acid (pyruvate) to the corresponding alpha-amino acid (alanine).
reactions.reasoning: Iron-catalyzed reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine has been experimentally demonstrated by Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019). The reaction proceeds at 70°C using elemental iron as reductant and ammonia/hydroxylamine as nitrogen source. This is part of a broader proto-metabolic network where alpha-keto acids are interconverted with amino acids. Alanine is one of the most abundant amino acids in prebiotic chemistry experiments and is the direct precursor for the aldol route to threonine (Aubrey et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of L-alanine with formaldehyde to L-threonine

conditions.other: Submarine hydrothermal vent conditions; elevated temperature and pressure drive the aldol condensation

reactions.mechanism: Aldol-type condensation: the alpha-carbon of L-alanine is deprotonated and attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of formaldehyde, forming a new C–C bond and introducing a beta-hydroxyl group (-CH(OH)-). This converts the C3 amino acid (alanine) into the C4 amino acid (threonine) by extending the carbon skeleton by one unit. The reaction is directly analogous to the glycine + formaldehyde → serine aldol condensation.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated by Aubrey, Cleaves, and Bada (2008, Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres) under simulated submarine hydrothermal vent conditions. This reaction establishes a hydrothermal amino acid interconversion network where simpler amino acids serve as building blocks for more complex ones through aldol chemistry with formaldehyde. While no specific mineral catalyst was documented for this particular step, the hydrothermal environment provides mineral surfaces (iron-sulfide, iron-oxide) that likely facilitate the reaction. The reaction may produce both threonine and allo-threonine stereoisomers.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: Thermal decarboxylation under mild hydrothermal or geothermal conditions

reactions.mechanism: Non-oxidative decarboxylation of pyruvate: loss of CO2 from the carboxyl group of pyruvate yields acetaldehyde. The reaction is thermally driven and proceeds readily at elevated temperatures. The resulting acetaldehyde retains the C2 unit and the aldehyde functionality needed for Strecker-type chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde is a well-established reaction that occurs readily under prebiotic conditions. This reaction links the hydrothermal pyruvate hub to the surface cyanosulfidic alanine synthesis pathway. Acetaldehyde is identified as the starting aldehyde for alanine production via the Strecker/cyanosulfidic route in the Patel et al. (2015) framework, where acetaldehyde + HCN produces the aminonitrile precursor to alanine.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of 2-aminopropanenitrile from acetaldehyde

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic conditions in surface evaporitic pools; sequential cyanohydrin formation and amination

reactions.mechanism: Classic Strecker synthesis: (1) HCN adds to the aldehyde carbonyl of acetaldehyde to form a cyanohydrin intermediate (lactonitrile), (2) ammonia displaces the hydroxyl group to form 2-aminopropanenitrile (alpha-aminonitrile). This two-step sequence converts a C2 aldehyde into a C3 aminonitrile bearing the alanine carbon skeleton.
reactions.reasoning: The cyanosulfidic production of alanine from acetaldehyde via aminonitrile is part of the unified protometabolism described by Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry). The same framework that produces threonine from lactaldehyde also produces alanine from acetaldehyde, demonstrating how a single chemical logic (aldehyde + HCN → cyanohydrin → aminonitrile → amino acid) generates the amino acid repertoire. This provides a surface-environment alternative route to alanine that complements the hydrothermal reductive amination (rxn_009).

reactions.name: Nitrile hydrolysis of 2-aminopropanenitrile to L-alanine

conditions.other: Aqueous hydrolysis in surface pools; wet-dry cycling may concentrate aminonitrile before hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydrolysis: water attacks the nitrile carbon of 2-aminopropanenitrile, forming 2-aminopropanamide as an intermediate. Further hydrolysis yields alanine with release of ammonia. This is the same nitrile-to-carboxylic-acid conversion as in rxn_008 but applied to the C3 aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids is the terminal step of the cyanosulfidic amino acid synthesis described by Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry). This reaction provides a second, independent route to L-alanine (complementing the hydrothermal reductive amination in rxn_009), establishing alanine as a convergence point in the network. The released ammonia is recycled for further amination reactions.

pathways.name: Full cyanosulfidic surface pathway to threonine
pathways.description: Complete surface-environment route from CO2/H2 through formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, lactaldehyde, and cyanosulfidic nitrile chemistry to threonine. Formaldehyde is produced hydrothermally and transported to surface pools where formose and cyanosulfidic chemistry operate.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal aldol pathway via reductive amination of pyruvate
pathways.description: Purely hydrothermal route: CO2 fixation produces pyruvate and formaldehyde; pyruvate undergoes iron-catalyzed reductive amination to alanine; alanine condenses with formaldehyde via aldol reaction to produce threonine.

pathways.name: Cross-environment pathway: hydrothermal pyruvate → surface cyanosulfidic alanine → hydrothermal aldol threonine
pathways.description: Pyruvate produced hydrothermally is decarboxylated to acetaldehyde, which enters surface cyanosulfidic chemistry to produce alanine via Strecker synthesis. Alanine is then transported back to hydrothermal setting for aldol condensation with formaldehyde to produce threonine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic alanine to hydrothermal aldol threonine (short cross-environment)
pathways.description: L-alanine produced by surface cyanosulfidic Strecker synthesis (from acetaldehyde + HCN + NH3) is transported to hydrothermal environment for aldol condensation with formaldehyde.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal C1 fixation to surface cyanosulfidic threonine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction produces formate and formaldehyde as C1 building blocks. Formaldehyde is transported to surface pools where formose chemistry and cyanosulfidic nitrile pathways construct threonine via lactaldehyde.

pathways.name: Pyruvate hub to alanine via hydrothermal reductive amination
pathways.description: Focused pathway from the pyruvate hub molecule through iron-catalyzed reductive amination to alanine, then aldol condensation with formaldehyde to threonine. Highlights the role of pyruvate as a central metabolic hub.

pathways.name: Aminonitrile core pathway (cyanosulfidic threonine-specific)
pathways.description: The core three-step cyanosulfidic pathway from lactaldehyde to threonine: cyanohydrin formation, amination, and nitrile hydrolysis. This is the experimentally demonstrated Patel et al. (2015) pathway.

pathways.name: Pyruvate decarboxylation to cyanosulfidic alanine pathway
pathways.description: Pyruvate is thermally decarboxylated to acetaldehyde, which undergoes Strecker synthesis with HCN and NH3 in surface pools to produce the aminonitrile precursor of alanine. Hydrolysis yields alanine.

pathways.name: Dual C1 fixation to formose-cyanosulfidic threonine
pathways.description: Two parallel CO2 fixation reactions produce formaldehyde (Fischer-Tropsch) and formate (greigite). Formaldehyde drives formose chemistry to glycolaldehyde and then lactaldehyde, which enters the cyanosulfidic pathway. Demonstrates redundancy in C1 fixation.

pathways.name: Convergent dual-environment threonine via ammonia recycling
pathways.description: Ammonia released during nitrile hydrolysis (rxn_008 or rxn_013) is recycled into reductive amination (rxn_009) or amination (rxn_007) reactions, creating a catalytic ammonia cycle. This pathway emphasizes NH3 as a hub molecule connecting cyanosulfidic and hydrothermal environments in threonine production.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong cyanosulfidic pathway directly aligned with Patel et al. (2015). The formose-based production of lactaldehyde (rxn_004, rxn_005) and the Strecker-cyanohydrin sequence (rxn_006-008) are well-supported. The weakest link is rxn_002 (CO2 + H2 → formaldehyde as a single hydrothermal step), which oversimplifies the FTT process. The surface reactions appropriately operate under cyanosulfidic conditions but would benefit from explicit mention of UV irradiation or mineral catalysts for the formose steps (e.g., CaCO3 or borate minerals to stabilize sugars).
suggestions: Add an intermediate CO step between CO2 and formaldehyde for greater chemical accuracy
Specify mineral catalysts for formose reactions (e.g., borate minerals to stabilize glycolaldehyde)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway captures the Aubrey et al. (2008) aldol condensation as the final step, which is well-supported. However, rxn_003 (CO2 + H2 → pyruvate in one step) is a major oversimplification; pyruvate formation from CO2 requires multiple intermediate steps (e.g., through CO, acetyl-CoA analogs, or glyoxylate). The reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine (rxn_009) is supported by Muchowska et al. (2019) but requires specification of iron catalysts. The aldol condensation mechanism (rxn_010) is debatable—formation of threonine from alanine + formaldehyde requires C-C bond formation at the beta-carbon, which is mechanistically unusual without enzymatic assistance.
suggestions: Break rxn_003 into multiple intermediate steps through CO, formate, acetyl thioester, and pyruvate
Specify Fe0/Fe2+ catalysts for rxn_009 as per Muchowska et al.
Discuss regiochemistry of rxn_010 more carefully

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This cross-environment pathway nicely demonstrates the Hydrothermal → Biochemical → Surface → Hydrothermal flow. The Strecker synthesis of alanine via acetaldehyde (rxn_012, rxn_013) is well-established prebiotically. The environmental transitions are justified: pyruvate from hydrothermal CO2 fixation, decarboxylation at moderate temperatures, Strecker chemistry in surface pools, then aldol condensation back in hydrothermal systems. The same issues with rxn_003 apply here.
suggestions: Note that mol_016 formula C3H4N2 should be C3H6N2 for 2-aminopropanenitrile

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Short cross-environment pathway from cyanosulfidic alanine synthesis to hydrothermal aldol threonine. Each step is individually well-supported. The environmental transition from surface Strecker chemistry to hydrothermal aldol is physically plausible via ocean transport.
suggestions: Specify transport mechanism between surface and hydrothermal environments

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The longest pathway, tracing from hydrothermal C1 fixation all the way to threonine via surface cyanosulfidic chemistry. Well-connected and demonstrates the full environmental progression. The inclusion of both rxn_001 (formate) and rxn_002 (formaldehyde) from hydrothermal sources feeding into surface formose chemistry is a strong design choice.
suggestions: Consider including a formate → formaldehyde reduction step to better connect rxn_001 with downstream chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A sub-pathway focused on the pyruvate-to-alanine hub connection. While the reductive amination is sound, this pathway alone doesn't reach threonine without rxn_010 being appended. The rxn_003 oversimplification limits confidence. This serves more as a network segment than a complete pathway.
suggestions: Better as a supporting sub-network than a standalone pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The core Patel et al. (2015) aminonitrile pathway: cyanohydrin formation → amination → hydrolysis. All three reactions are experimentally demonstrated and mechanistically well-understood. This is the most chemically robust sub-pathway in the network. Operates entirely in the surface cyanosulfidic environment, which is consistent with the literature.
suggestions: Could note that UV irradiation may assist the hydrolysis step under cyanosulfidic conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Pyruvate decarboxylation to acetaldehyde followed by Strecker synthesis of alanine is chemically sound and shows good environmental flow (Hydrothermal → Biochemical → Surface). However, this pathway only produces alanine, not threonine, so target achievement is incomplete unless combined with rxn_010.
suggestions: Extend pathway to include the final aldol step to threonine

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Dual C1 fixation (formate and formaldehyde) feeding into the formose-cyanosulfidic route. Nicely demonstrates how multiple hydrothermal C1 products converge on surface chemistry. The formate production (rxn_001) on greigite is well-established, and the formose-to-cyanosulfidic sequence is the core strength of this network.
suggestions: Add a formate-to-formaldehyde reduction step to better connect the two C1 fixation products

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Shows the convergent dual-environment production of threonine with ammonia recycling between nitrile hydrolysis (which releases NH3) and reductive amination (which consumes NH3). This is an elegant network feature that demonstrates self-sustaining nitrogen cycling. Both threonine-producing reactions are literature-supported.
suggestions: Explicitly quantify ammonia fluxes to demonstrate recycling feasibility

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-constructed synthesis network for L-threonine that faithfully incorporates both major literature-supported prebiotic routes: the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic cyanohydrin/aminonitrile pathway and the Aubrey et al. (2008) hydrothermal aldol condensation. The network topology is strong, with 16 molecules, 13 reactions, 4 hub molecules, and 5 convergence points creating a genuinely interconnected system rather than just parallel linear chains. Key strengths include: (1) excellent literature alignment with both primary experimental references, (2) good environmental flow from hydrothermal C1 fixation through surface cyanosulfidic chemistry to the target, (3) ammonia recycling between pathways demonstrating metabolic-like network behavior, and (4) multiple independent routes providing robustness. Key weaknesses are: (1) rxn_003 (CO2 + H2 → pyruvate) is a gross oversimplification of what is a multi-step process, reducing confidence in pathways that depend on it; (2) rxn_002 (CO2 → formaldehyde via FTT) would benefit from an intermediate CO step; (3) mol_016 has an incorrect molecular formula (C3H4N2 should be C3H6N2 for 2-aminopropanenitrile); (4) mineral catalysts are not consistently specified for hydrothermal and surface reactions (only rxn_001 mentions greigite). The core cyanosulfidic chemistry (P7: rxn_006 → rxn_007 → rxn_008) is the strongest and most experimentally grounded segment. Despite the noted issues with upstream C1 fixation, the network achieves its stated goal of providing convergent, multi-environment prebiotic routes to L-threonine with good chemical fidelity.
